title: Fables – Young Children (Ages 6–8)

hunks.text: A FABLE is a short story that teaches you a lesson about how to act or behave. Fables are very old—people have been telling them for thousands of years! Most FABLES have ANIMAL CHARACTERS in them, like foxes, ants, or birds. These animals talk and act like people do. When you read a fable, you learn something important about being kind, honest, or brave. The lesson is called a MORAL, and it helps you understand the right way to do things.

questions.question: What is a fable?
questions.answer: A SHORT STORY THAT TEACHES YOU A LESSON ABOUT HOW TO ACT OR BEHAVE

questions.question: Can you think of an animal character you know from a story?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE (EXAMPLES: A MOUSE, A BEAR, A CAT, A LION)

questions.question: Name two things the text tells us about fables.
questions.answer: THEY ARE SHORT STORIES / THEY TEACH LESSONS / THEY HAVE ANIMAL CHARACTERS / THEY ARE VERY OLD / THEY HELP YOU UNDERSTAND THE RIGHT WAY TO DO THINGS

questions.question: What is the lesson in a fable called?
questions.answer: A MORAL

questions.question: Imagine an animal that can talk. What animal would it be, and what would you hear it say?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE (EXAMPLES: A FOX SAYING 'HELLO' / A BIRD SINGING WORDS / AN ANT TALKING ABOUT WORK)

hunks.text: One of the most famous fables ever written is called 'The Tortoise and the Hare.' In this story, a HARE (which is a fast animal like a rabbit) and a TORTOISE (which is a slow turtle) decide to race each other. The hare is so fast that he thinks he will win easily, so he stops to nap during the race. The slow tortoise keeps moving and moving without stopping. When the hare wakes up, the tortoise has already crossed the finish line! The MORAL of this fable is that working hard and never giving up can be better than being naturally fast or smart.

questions.question: In 'The Tortoise and the Hare,' which animal is fast?
questions.answer: THE HARE

questions.question: What does the hare do during the race?
questions.answer: HE STOPS TO NAP / HE TAKES A SLEEP

questions.question: Name two things that happen in 'The Tortoise and the Hare.'
questions.answer: A HARE AND TORTOISE DECIDE TO RACE / THE HARE THINKS HE WILL WIN EASILY / THE HARE STOPS TO NAP / THE TORTOISE KEEPS MOVING / THE TORTOISE CROSSES THE FINISH LINE FIRST

questions.question: Do you think the tortoise is braver than the hare? Why or why not?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE

questions.question: If the hare and tortoise raced for ten minutes, but the hare napped for seven of those minutes, how many minutes was the hare actually racing?
questions.answer: 3 MINUTES

hunks.text: Another beloved fable is 'The Ant and the Grasshopper.' In this story, an ANT works very hard all summer long, gathering food and preparing for winter. A GRASSHOPPER spends all summer playing music and having fun instead of working. When cold winter arrives, the ant has plenty of food to eat, but the grasshopper has nothing. The grasshopper asks the ant for help, and the kind ant shares food with him. The MORAL teaches us that it is important to prepare for the future and work when you should, but also shows us that kindness matters too.

questions.question: What does the ant do during summer?
questions.answer: THE ANT WORKS VERY HARD / GATHERS FOOD / PREPARES FOR WINTER

questions.question: What does the grasshopper do during summer?
questions.answer: THE GRASSHOPPER PLAYS MUSIC / HAS FUN

questions.question: What season comes after summer?
questions.answer: FALL / AUTUMN (FOLLOWED BY WINTER)

questions.question: Name two things the moral of 'The Ant and the Grasshopper' teaches us.
questions.answer: IT IS IMPORTANT TO PREPARE FOR THE FUTURE / IT IS IMPORTANT TO WORK WHEN YOU SHOULD / KINDNESS MATTERS

questions.question: Close your eyes and imagine being an ant working hard in the warm sun. What do you feel, and what do you hear around you?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE (EXAMPLES: WARMTH / DIRT / RUSTLING / INSECTS / WORKING SOUNDS)

hunks.text: In fables, the ANIMAL CHARACTERS always have the same kinds of PERSONALITY TRAITS. A fox is always clever and tricky. A lion is always brave and strong. An owl is always wise. A donkey is always stubborn. These TRAITS stay the same from one fable to another, so when you see a fox in a story, you know it will be smart and sneaky! This makes fables easy to understand because the animals are PREDICTABLE. You can guess what they might do next based on their nature.

questions.question: What is a fox like in fables?
questions.answer: CLEVER AND TRICKY

questions.question: Name two animal characters and their personality traits from the text.
questions.answer: A FOX IS CLEVER AND TRICKY / A LION IS BRAVE AND STRONG / AN OWL IS WISE / A DONKEY IS STUBBORN

questions.question: What word means that animal characters stay the same from one fable to another?
questions.answer: PREDICTABLE

questions.question: If you were an animal character in a fable, what personality traits would you have?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE (EXAMPLES: KIND / FUNNY / BRAVE / HELPFUL)

questions.question: Picture a clever fox sneaking through the woods. What do you see the fox doing with its tail and ears?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE (EXAMPLES: TAIL SWISHING / EARS PERKED UP / MOVING QUIETLY / LOOKING AROUND)

hunks.text: One of the oldest fables in the world comes from a man named AESOP, who lived in ancient Greece many, many years ago. AESOP'S FABLES have been told and retold for over two thousand years! Some of his most famous stories are 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf,' 'The Tortoise and the Hare,' and 'The Lion and the Mouse.' Even though these stories are very, very old, children all around the world still love reading them today. They teach TIMELESS lessons about honesty, hard work, and kindness that never get old.

questions.question: Who was Aesop?
questions.answer: A MAN WHO LIVED IN ANCIENT GREECE / A FAMOUS FABLE WRITER

questions.question: If Aesop's fables have been told for over two thousand years, is that longer or shorter than one hundred years?
questions.answer: LONGER (TWO THOUSAND IS MORE THAN ONE HUNDRED)

questions.question: Name two of Aesop's famous fables from the text.
questions.answer: THE BOY WHO CRIED WOLF / THE TORTOISE AND THE HARE / THE LION AND THE MOUSE

questions.question: What does it mean that Aesop's lessons are timeless?
questions.answer: THE LESSONS NEVER GET OLD / THEY ARE STILL IMPORTANT TODAY

questions.question: What country is Greece?
questions.answer: A COUNTRY IN EUROPE

hunks.text: In 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf,' a young shepherd boy gets bored watching his sheep all day. He decides to play a trick and yells, 'Wolf! Wolf!' even though there is no wolf. All the villagers come running to help him, but when they see there is no wolf, they are angry and leave. The boy plays this trick many times. Then one day, a real wolf comes to eat the sheep, and the boy yells for help again. But this time, nobody comes because they don't believe him anymore. The MORAL teaches that if you tell lies, people will not trust you when you tell the truth.

questions.question: Why does the shepherd boy yell 'Wolf' the first time?
questions.answer: HE IS BORED / HE PLAYS A TRICK / TO PLAY A JOKE

questions.question: What happens when a real wolf comes?
questions.answer: NOBODY COMES TO HELP / PEOPLE DON'T BELIEVE HIM / THE WOLF EATS THE SHEEP

questions.question: Name two events that happen in 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf.'
questions.answer: THE BOY IS BORED WATCHING SHEEP / HE YELLS 'WOLF' AND TRICKS PEOPLE / THE VILLAGERS GET ANGRY / HE PLAYS THE TRICK MANY TIMES / A REAL WOLF COMES / NOBODY COMES TO HELP

questions.question: Do you think the boy was wrong to play tricks? What would you do if you were bored watching sheep?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE

questions.question: Imagine you hear someone yelling for help. How would your body feel, and what would you do?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE (EXAMPLES: SCARED / WORRIED / RUN TO HELP / LISTEN CAREFULLY)

hunks.text: Fables teach lessons through STORYTELLING, which means they show you a story instead of just telling you what to do. Rather than saying, 'You should be honest,' a fable tells you a story about what happens when someone is dishonest, and you learn the lesson by watching the characters. This way of teaching makes the lesson MEMORABLE because you remember the story better than you remember a rule. When you read or hear fables, your brain is busy imagining the animals, the action, and the problem they face. This ENGAGEMENT makes learning fun and helps the lesson stick with you for a long time.

questions.question: How do fables teach lessons?
questions.answer: THROUGH STORYTELLING / BY SHOWING YOU A STORY

questions.question: Why are lessons in fables memorable?
questions.answer: YOU REMEMBER THE STORY BETTER THAN YOU REMEMBER A RULE

questions.question: Name two things your brain does when you read a fable.
questions.answer: YOU IMAGINE THE ANIMALS / YOU IMAGINE THE ACTION / YOU IMAGINE THE PROBLEM / YOU LEARN THE LESSON

questions.question: Close your eyes and imagine watching a fable story. What animal do you picture, and what is it doing?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE (EXAMPLES: A TURTLE MOVING SLOWLY / A FOX RUNNING / A BIRD FLYING)

questions.question: What story have you heard that taught you a lesson? What was the lesson?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE

hunks.text: Today, fables are still written and shared by STORYTELLERS, teachers, and authors all around the world. You can read fables in books, watch them as ANIMATED VIDEOS, or listen to them told aloud. Some modern authors write NEW FABLES using animals or even people as characters to teach lessons about friendship, being brave, or protecting the environment. No matter how old or new a fable is, it always teaches a MORAL that helps you understand how to live better. The most important thing about fables is that they remind us that our choices matter and that we can learn from the choices other characters make.

questions.question: Name three ways you can enjoy fables today.
questions.answer: READ THEM IN BOOKS / WATCH ANIMATED VIDEOS / LISTEN TO THEM TOLD ALOUD

questions.question: Name two topics that modern fables might teach about.
questions.answer: FRIENDSHIP / BEING BRAVE / PROTECTING THE ENVIRONMENT

questions.question: What do fables remind us about?
questions.answer: OUR CHOICES MATTER / WE CAN LEARN FROM THE CHOICES OTHER CHARACTERS MAKE

questions.question: If you could write your own fable, what lesson would you want to teach?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE (EXAMPLES: BE KIND / WORK HARD / TELL THE TRUTH / HELP OTHERS)

questions.question: Imagine watching an animated fable on a screen. What colors and movements do you see?
questions.answer: STUDENT CHOICE (EXAMPLES: BRIGHT COLORS / ANIMALS MOVING / GRASS / SKY)

citations: Aesop's Fables: The Classic Collection Illustrated. Dover Publications, 2015.
Pinkney, Jerry. The Lion and the Mouse. Little, Brown Books for Young Readers, 2015.
National Center for Children's Illustrated Literature (NCCIL). 'Fables Around the World.' University of Waco, accessed 2025.
